g.data-narration: The Bitcoin network is a decentralized peer-to-peer system where all nodes are equal participants without a central authority.
g.text: Decentralized peer-to-peer network enabling value transfer without central authority.
All nodes are equal participants maintaining consensus on the blockchain.

g.data-narration: Key characteristics include decentralization, P2P mesh architecture, permissionless participation, and open source transparency.
Bitcoin has several node types: Full nodes validate all rules, mining nodes create blocks, and SPV nodes provide lightweight verification.
Full nodes are the network backbone - they validate all transactions and blocks, enforcing consensus rules independently.
SPV nodes download only block headers and use Merkle proofs to verify transactions without validating all network rules.
Mining nodes bundle transactions into blocks and solve Proof-of-Work to create new bitcoins through specialized hardware.
Transactions broadcast from wallet to peers, get validated and relayed through the network via gossip protocol until mined.
A Bitcoin wallet stores cryptographic keys, not actual bitcoin. Bitcoin exists as records on the blockchain.
g.text: A wallet does NOT store bitcoin - it stores keys that control bitcoin on the blockchain.
The wallet holds private keys enabling spending, while bitcoin exists as immutable ledger records.

g.data-narration: Private keys are secret 256-bit numbers controlling funds. Public keys derive from private keys, and addresses encode public keys.
Private keys generate public keys via ECDSA elliptic curve cryptography. Addresses derive from public keys through SHA-256 and RIPEMD-160 hashing.
Custodial wallets let third parties hold your keys like a bank. Non-custodial wallets give you sole control - not your keys, not your coins.
Hot wallets connect to the internet for convenience but face online threats. Cold wallets stay offline for maximum security.
HD wallets generate unlimited keys from a single 12 or 24-word seed phrase, enabling simple backup and recovery.
Transaction lifecycle: Creation, signing with private key, then broadcast to the P2P network for propagation.
Transactions enter mempool, get mined into a block, then confirmed on blockchain with increasing immutability.
The Bitcoin P2P network operates through full nodes validating transactions, SPV nodes for lightweight access, and miners creating blocks.
Bitcoin wallets manage keys - not coins. Use non-custodial, cold storage for security. HD wallets simplify backup with seed phrases.
For exams: Know differences between full/SPV nodes, hot/cold wallets, and the transaction lifecycle from creation to confirmation.
g.text: • Distinguish full nodes vs SPV nodes | • Hot vs Cold wallet security trade-offs | • "Not your keys, not your coins" | • Know transaction lifecycle steps
